To 
: File 
Date 
5-6-77 
From 
Subject: 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ted Prim 
Question from Santa Clara City Attorney Ed Moore concerning 
disqualification of City Councilmember on votes which fore-
seeably and materially may benefit Peak's Travel Agency, a 
source of income to her. 
On May 2, 1977, Mr. Moore called to ask whether a Santa 
Clara City Councilwoman must 
disqualify herself from votes 
which may affect Peak's Travel Agency. 
The. Councibloman 
is an inde?endent contractor who receives commissions in 
the amount of approximately $2,000 per year for referring 
clients to Peak's. 
The City has historically booked most 
of its a 
transportation with Peak's although there is no 
formal policy to 
s effect. 
Last year Peak's received 
$13,000 in business from the City of Santa Clara of which 
their commission was 8%. 
We discussed three types of votes in which the Councilwoman 
might wish to participate: 
One, votes on the City travel 
budget; two, votes approving specific travel 
for City 
officials, e.g., should planning commissioners be sent to 
a conference; three, approving bills submitted by City of-
ficials for previous travel. 
With respect to the first type of vote, I advised him that 
it appears that a vote on the City travel budget may fore-
seeably affect all four travel agencies in Santa Clara in-
cluding Peak's. 
However, since Peak's generally has garnered 
most of the City's business, it is likely that decisions on 
the City travel budget will affect Peak's more than the other 
three travel agencies. 
Thus, the question becomes whether 
this foreseeable effect is material. 
Since Peak's received 
commissions of approximately $1,000 last year, the question 
is whether, pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, this 
amount represents 1 
of the gross revenues or 1/2 of 1% of 
the net profits of Peak. 
I also informed him of the subjec-
tive standard for find 
materiality whenever there is rea-
son to believe that the official is biased. 
Accordingly, I 
indicated that there was a distinct possibility that she 


File 
2 
5-6-77 
should disqualify herself from participating in decisions 
affecting the travel budget for the City of Santa Clara. 
with respect to the second and third types of dbcisions, it 
appears most unlikely that the relevant threshold would be 
triggered. 
Thus, unless the sUbjective standard were applica-
ble, she would probably be able to participate in these types 
of decisions. 
TP:jo

sections.conclusion: With respect to votes on the City travel budget: it appears that such a vote may foreseeably affect Peak's Travel Agency. Since Peak's generally has garnered most of the City's business, it is likely that decisions on the City travel budget will affect Peak's more than other travel agencies. The question becomes whether this foreseeable effect is material pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. The subjective standard for materiality applies when there is reason to believe the official is biased. Accordingly, there is a distinct possibility that she should disqualify herself from participating in decisions affecting the travel budget. With respect to votes on specific travel and approving bills for previous travel: it appears most unlikely that the relevant threshold would be triggered, and unless the subjective standard were applicable, she would probably be able to participate in these types of decisions.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a city councilwoman disqualify herself from voting on matters that could foreseeably and materially benefit Peak's Travel Agency, from which she receives approximately $2,000 annually in referral commissions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The councilwoman likely should disqualify herself from votes on the City travel budget since Peak's receives the majority of the City's travel business and the foreseeable effect may be material. However, she would probably be able to participate in votes on specific travel arrangements or bills for previous travel unless the subjective materiality standard applies based on evidence of bias.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality document with some formatting issues. Verbatim question and conclusion sections extracted. Document is internal FPPC memorandum to file dated 5-6-77 with clear question and detailed advice sections. Minor OCR artifacts present but content is coherent and analyzable.

disqualification of City Councilmember on votes which fore-
seeably and materially may benefit Peak's Travel Agency, a

On May 2, 1977, Mr. Moore called to ask whether a Santa 
Clara City Councilwoman must 
disqualify herself from votes 
which may affect Peak's Travel Agency. 
The. Councibloman 
is an inde?endent contractor who receives commissions in

clients to Peak's. 
The City has historically booked most

Last year Peak's received 
$13,000 in business from the City of Santa Clara of which

We discussed three types of votes in which the Councilwoman 
might wish to participate: 
One, votes on the City travel 
budget; two, votes approving specific travel 
for City 
officials, e.g., should planning commissioners be sent to 
a conference; three, approving bills submitted by City of-
ficials for previous travel. 
With respect to the first type of vote, I advised him that 
it appears that a vote on the City travel budget may fore-

cluding Peak's.

should disqualify herself from participating in decisions 
affecting the travel budget for the City of Santa Clara. 
with respect to the second and third types of dbcisions, it 
appears most unlikely that the relevant threshold would be

TP:jo

CONCLUSION: With respect to votes on the City travel budget: it appears that such a vote may foreseeably affect Peak's Travel Agency. Since Peak's generally has garnered most of the City's business, it is likely that decisions on the City travel budget will affect Peak's more than other travel agencies. The question becomes whether this foreseeable effect is material pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. The subjective standard for materiality applies when there is reason to believe the official is biased. Accordingly, there is a distinct possibility that she should disqualify herself from participating in decisions affecting the travel budget. With respect to votes on specific travel and approving bills for previous travel: it appears most unlikely that the relevant threshold would be triggered, and unless the subjective standard were applicable, she would probably be able to participate in these types of decisions.

embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum responding to inquiry from Santa Clara City Attorney regarding whether a city councilwoman must disqualify herself from voting on matters affecting Peak's Travel Agency, her source of approximately $2,000 annual income. FPPC advises probable disqualification from travel budget votes but likely participation in votes on specific travel matters and bills.
